NVIDIA Blackwell Supply Constraints Worse Than Expected, Pushing Deliveries to Late 2025

By Kevin Lam | March 6, 2025 | Semiconductor Engineering

NVIDIA's next-generation Blackwell B200 GPU is facing more severe supply constraints than previously communicated, according to multiple industry sources familiar with TSMC's production schedule.

Initial Blackwell volume production, originally targeted for Q2 2025, has been pushed back by approximately 2-3 months due to yield issues with TSMC's CoWoS-L advanced packaging technology. Sources indicate current yields are running at 50-60%, below the 70%+ target needed for volume production.

The delay means that several hyperscale customers including Microsoft, Google, and Amazon will receive their initial Blackwell orders later than planned, potentially impacting their AI infrastructure deployment timelines for 2025.

"Supply constraints create a double-edged sword for NVIDIA," said Chris Caso, analyst at Wolfe Research. "On one hand, it limits near-term revenue upside. On the other hand, it means there's a massive pent-up demand backlog that extends revenue visibility well into 2026."

NVIDIA has not officially confirmed the delay but acknowledged during its Q4 earnings call that "demand significantly exceeds supply" for Blackwell. The company reportedly has over $30 billion in Blackwell orders that have not yet been fulfilled.

Some customers are reportedly extending their purchases of the current-generation H200 GPU to bridge the gap, which could help maintain NVIDIA's data-center revenue in the interim but at potentially lower margins than Blackwell.

TSMC is investing $2.8 billion to expand CoWoS packaging capacity at its new Kumamoto, Japan facility, but the additional capacity is not expected to come online until early 2026.

NVIDIA shares fell 1.5% on the report. AMD shares rose 2.8% as investors bet that supply constraints could accelerate customer interest in the MI350 as an alternative.
